P.O. Box 807 o 428 J Street r Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
May 23,2002
Linda W. Dixon
260 Princeton Drive
Costa Mesa, CA 92626
Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-02-098
Dear Ms. Dixon
This is in response to your request for informal assistance regarding the conflict
of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").l Since you are not
inquiring about a particular governmental decision, we are treating your request as one
for informal assistance. 
2
QUESTION
Will your position as mayor of Costa Mesa create a conflict of interest if you are
appointed to the Orange County Fairgrounds Board of Directors?
CONCLUSION
No. Your positions as mayor of the City of Costa Mesa and as a member of the
Orange County Fairgrounds Board of Directors are not economic interests which could
be affected by decisions of either the Orange County Fairgrounds Board of Directors or
the City of Costa Mesa.
FACTS
You are presently the mayor of the City of Costa Mesa. You are contemplating
seeking appointment to the Orange County Fairgrounds Board of Directors and are
inquiring whether a conflict of interest exists should you be appointed.
rGovernment Code sections 81000-91014. Commissionregulations appear atTitle 2, sections
18109-18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations.
2Pnrsuant to enclosed Regulation 15329(c)(2), informal assistance does not confer immunity.
t:


File No. I-02-098
Page2
ANALYSIS
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial
interests...." (Section 81001.) Specifically, Section 87100 prohibits any public official
from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision within the
meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will
have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests.
(Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) Among other things, an economic interest is any
source of income, including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within
12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3). However,
income does not include:
"Salary3 and reimbursement for expenses or per diem
received from a state, local, or federal government agency
and reimbursement for travel expenses and per diem
received from a bona fide nonprofit entity exempt from
taxation under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue
Code. " (S ection 82030(b)(2).) (Footnote added.)
Consequently, you do not have an economic interest in the City of Costa Mesa,
nor do you have an economic interest in the Orange County Fairgrounds, Since a conflict
of interest will arise only when it is reasonably foreseeable that a decision will have a
material financial effect on a public official's economic interest4, your governmental
positions do not create a conflict of interest under the Act.
This agency only administers the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act. Your
more specific question concerns the doctrine of incompatible public offices. This
' "'salary' from a state, local, or federal governrnent agency means any and all payments made by
a governrnent agency to a public official, or accrued to the benefit ofa public official, as consideration for
the public offrcial's services to the government agency. Suchpayments include wages, fees paid to public
officials as 'consultants' as defined in California Code of Regulations, Title2, section 18701(a)(2), pension
benefits, health and other insurance coverage, rights to compensated vacation and leave time, free or
discounted transportation, payment or indemnification of legal defense costs, and similar benefits."
(Re gulation 1823 2 (a) .)
a In addition to the economic interests separately listed in section 87103, a public official always
has an economic interest in his or her personal finances, and may have a conflict of interest in any decision
foreseeably resulting in an increase or decrease in the personal expenses, income, assets or liabilities ofthe
official or his or her immediate family, in the amount of $250 or more over a l2-month period. @egulation
18703.5.) When the only potentially disqualifying economic interest is governmenlal salary as defined in
section 820300)(2) and regulation 18232, no potentially disqualifying economic interest exists under either
section 87103(c) or the personal financial effects mle described in regulation 18703.5, However,
governrnental salary is a disqualiS,ing economic interest if there is a unique effect on the official or his or
her inrmediate family as described in regulation 18705.50).


File No. I-02-098
Page 3
doctrine is not implemented or interpreted by the Act or the Fair Political Practices
Commission.
This question should be directed to the Office of the Attorney General's public
inquiry unit at (800) 952-5225. To assist you in understanding the Act's conflict-of-
interest provisions should you be appointed to Orange County Fairgrounds Board of
Directors, I have enclosed afact sheet entitled, "Holding Two Positions."
If you have any questions, please feel free to contact me at (866) 275-3772.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel

By: Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Technical Assistance Division

QUESTION: Will your position as mayor of Costa Mesa create a conflict of interest if you are
appointed to the Orange County Fairgrounds Board of Directors?

CONCLUSION: No. Your positions as mayor of the City of Costa Mesa and as a member of the
Orange County Fairgrounds Board of Directors are not economic interests which could
be affected by decisions of either the Orange County Fairgrounds Board of Directors or
the City of Costa Mesa.
